So what's going on here? Let's look at how
static method creates a new object, using an existing object as the prototype of the newly created object.
Okay, I know that doesn't read like plain English. In plain terms: it takes the object and properties you pass in, sets them as the

of a new object, and creates that new object. So what does that buy us? It's useful for inheritance — the "B inherits from A" idea. If inheritance still feels fuzzy, picture being born into the world: you inherit traits from your parents — maybe your dad is taller, maybe your mom has longer eyelashes, and so on. That's inheritance. Enough talk — let's just start coding, it's cleaner that way.

The code will still run fine even if you don't put

points to the constructor function itself, so we normally put it back to keep the prototype complete. The main point is to "correctly mark which function produced this object" — otherwise, when you're tracing down a problem during future development, you won't be able to figure out what's going on.

In this chapter we'll build our own prototype chain from scratch. We'll start with a simple constructor, see why it falls short, then use Object.create to set up multi-level inheritance — Dog inheriting from Animal. Along the way we'll cover why you still need call() to inherit the constructor, and why you have to put back Dog.prototype.constructor to keep the prototype chain intact.
